benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: 25-Jun official cash $3,132.00 (Westmetall), down $18.00 (-0.6%) vs 24-Jun $3,150.00 — a fourth straight down-day extending the correction to a ~4-month low (last seen late-Feb). The Gulf war premium that drove the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) has fully unwound: the US-Iran ceasefire/MoU holds, Hormuz transit is normal and Brent sits near a ~4-month low. avg = May-2026 monthly mean ($3,670.18).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 25-Jun official 3M $3,146.00 vs 24-Jun $3,160.50 (-$14.50) — a fresh ~4-month low. A firm dollar (DXY ~101.5, a 13-month high), rising China/Indonesia output and soft China demand cap any rebound. avg = May-2026 mean ($3,600.79).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 25-Jun cash $3,132.00 vs 3M $3,146.00 = -$14.00 = CONTANGO, deepening from -$10.50 on 24-Jun. The acute front-end scarcity premium has fully unwound with the Gulf de-escalation; the curve signals eased near-term tightness even as the structural deficit persists further out.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: ~CNY 24,000/t SMM spot (incl-VAT) → USD excl-VAT equiv; easing with LME as the Gulf scare faded, though SHFE has been more resilient than LME on the way down — verify SMM/metal.com.

benchmark.name: LME inventory (tonnes)
benchmark.note: 25-Jun official 308,225 t (Westmetall), down 2,000 t vs 24-Jun (310,225 t); ~92% Russian-origin; ~1.3 days output. Draws continuing — the deficit is structural and unaffected by the price collapse.

macro_events: WAR PREMIUM FULLY UNWOUND — FOURTH STRAIGHT DOWN-DAY TO A ~4-MONTH LOW. The 25-Jun Westmetall official (latest confirmed) settled cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 (stock 308,225 t), down ~$18/$14.5 (-0.6%/-0.5%). With 24-Jun and 25-Jun officials now confirmed, the correction off the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855) measures ~-19% in three weeks. The cash–3M curve has deepened to a -$14 contango as front-end scarcity eased.

Hormuz transit normal: the US-Iran ceasefire/MoU holds, Iran's foreign ministry confirms shipping 'operating normally', CENTCOM reiterates Iran does not control the strait, and a 60-day Iran oil-export window is in force. Commercial traffic has fully normalised since the 17-Jun MoU; the acute corridor-closure narrative that drove the early-June spike is over.

Macro now drives the tape: the USD holds a 13-month high (DXY ~101.5) even as Fed Sept-hike odds ease to ~63% (from ~68%); Brent rose ~2% to ~$74.7 on 25-Jun but stays near a ~4-month low as Iranian barrels return. Rising China/Indonesia output and soft China demand cap rebounds.

Floor intact: Gulf capacity is still impaired (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial) and LME stock keeps drawing (308,225 t, ~92% Russian-origin) — the structural deficit underpins the $3,080–$3,150 support band the price has now entered.
lme_commentary: Commentary: the 25-Jun Westmetall official confirmed a fourth straight down-day — cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 (stock 308,225 t, -2,000 t), a ~4-month low. The war premium that drove the early-June run to a four-year high ($3,855) has fully collapsed: the US-Iran ceasefire/MoU held, Hormuz shipping is operating normally and a 60-day Iran oil-export window is in force. The cash–3M curve has deepened to a -$14 contango as front-end scarcity eased. Macro now drives the tape — the USD holds a 13-month high (DXY ~101.5), Brent sits near a ~4-month low (~$74.7) and China/Indonesia output is rising into soft China demand. The structural deficit is intact (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, LME stock still drawing) and provides a floor, but with the geopolitical catalyst gone the near-term bias stays lower toward the $3,080–$3,150 support band, which price has now entered.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$348–350/t FOB East Australia; Q2 oversupply pressuring

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region; steady on the building surplus

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$350/t) softer on Q2 oversupply vs Platts API region (~$305/t); a ~1.2 Mt 2026 surplus is building on higher production/shipments into subdued demand.
Hormuz reopening removes the brief Gulf alumina-logistics risk — ~60% of regional alumina imports transit the Strait, and flows have normalised since the 17-Jun MoU.

Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$66/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34); Guinea is weighing export curbs (a >$100/dmt target) — a structural upside risk to raw-material cost independent of the Gulf.
Refining: Gulf gas-supply risk receding with the strait reopening; EGA's Al Taweelah refinery restart timeline (~12 months) remains the key supply swing factor.
Net: alumina balance skews to surplus (ample supply caps the price); the de-escalation removes the logistics overlay, leaving Guinea bauxite-curb risk as the main floor under raw-material cost.
net_read: WAR PREMIUM FULLY GONE — PRICE AT A ~4-MONTH LOW, MACRO IN CONTROL. Aluminium's run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) was built on the Gulf supply shock (EGA Al Taweelah and ALBA outages, Strait-of-Hormuz disruption, ~3 Mt removed). That premium has now fully unwound and the slide has extended: the 25-Jun official (latest confirmed) settled cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 (Westmetall), down ~0.6%, a fourth straight down-day to a ~4-month low; the cash–3M curve has deepened to a -$14 contango. The US-Iran ceasefire/MoU holds, Hormuz transit runs normally, and Brent sits near a ~4-month low (~$74.7). A firm dollar (DXY ~101.5, a 13-month high), rising China/Indonesia output and soft China demand now dominate the tape. The structural-deficit floor stays intact — EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, LME stock drawing to 308,225 t (~92% Russian-origin, ~1.3 days output) — but with the acute logistics shock over the bullish-supply narrative has lost its catalyst; near-term bias stays lower toward the $3,080–$3,150 wave-C confluence, which price has now entered. US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated on the 50% Section 232 rate even as the LME falls.

feed.text: Aluminium extended its slide for a fourth straight session: 25-Jun official cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 (-0.6%/-0.5%), a ~4-month low. 24-Jun and 25-Jun officials are now confirmed (3,150.0/3,160.5 then 3,132.0/3,146.0), validating the continued unwind.
Cash–3M curve deepened to a -$14 contango (from -$10.5 on 24-Jun) as front-end scarcity kept easing — a clearer signal that near-term tightness has faded with Hormuz reopened.

Macro took control: the USD held a 13-month high (DXY ~101.5) even as Fed Sept-hike odds eased to ~63% (from ~68%); Brent rose ~2% to ~$74.7 but stayed near a ~4-month low as Iranian barrels return on the 60-day waiver.
Base-metals complex broadly soft: copper 3M $13,235 (stock drawing), nickel $16,820 near 2026 lows on surplus, zinc $3,425 — the whole board eased with a firmer dollar.

Gulf capacity still impaired — EGA Al Taweelah ~12 months to restore, ALBA partial — and LME stock keeps drawing (308,225 t, ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.3 days output): the deficit floor is intact even as the flat price falls.

news.headline: 25-Jun LME official (latest confirmed) extended lower to cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 (Westmetall), down ~0.6%/0.5% — a fourth straight down-day and a ~4-month low. Cash–3M deepened to a -$14 contango.

Hormuz transit remains normal: the US-Iran ceasefire/MoU holds, Iran's foreign ministry says shipping is 'operating normally', CENTCOM reiterates Iran does not control the strait, and a 60-day Iran oil-export window is in force. The acute corridor-closure narrative that drove the early-June spike is over.

25-Jun: the USD held a 13-month high (DXY ~101.5) with Fed Sept-hike odds easing to ~63%; Brent rose ~2% to ~$74.7 but stayed near a ~4-month low as Iranian barrels return — keeping $-priced metals capped.

Section 232 aluminium tariff stays at 50% on full customs value (proclamation eff. 8-Jun-2026, through 2027) — US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (MW US Transaction ~$2,420/t) even as the LME falls; EU 6% duty + CBAM underpin European premiums.
Gulf capacity still impaired (EGA Al Taweelah ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial) and LME stock keeps drawing to 308,225 t — the structural deficit provides a floor under the correction.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 26-Jun (no new releases this week). Q2 season begins mid/late-July: Alcoa confirmed 22-Jul (after close); Norsk Hydro late-Jul (has hedged a large share of 2026 primary output and premiums). Maaden (1211) reports Q2 in late-Jul/early-Aug. Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Alcircle, Discovery Alert, Investing.com.

commercial.text: The war premium has fully collapsed — the 25-Jun official extended to $3,132 cash / $3,146 3M (a ~4-month low) and the curve sits in a -$14 contango. Protect realisation: keep LME hedges firmly in place on priced volume and accelerate pricing on unpriced tonnes into any dead-cat bounce rather than waiting for a recovery that lacks a catalyst. As a non-disrupted supplier, defend value-added (billet/slab/PFA) premium capture even as the flat price falls; avoid chasing the move lower with discounts.

De-escalation keeps pressuring Asian premiums: CIF Japan spot is easing (~$345) and Q3 MJP negotiations now skew down toward ~$275–320 (from the spike-driven $340–360). Lock in remaining elevated premiums on renewals NOW before the Q3 settlement resets lower, while EGA/ALBA capacity stays impaired. US MW remains structurally high (~$2,420/t) on the 50% Section 232 tariff but tracks the lower LME via the tariff component.

Ex-China FOB Australia (~$350/t) vs softer API region (~$305/t); the Q2 surplus caps it and the Hormuz reopening removes the Gulf logistics risk. If net seller, transact into any firmness promptly given the surplus bias; if buyer for smelting, the surplus offers softer windows — favourable input-cost backdrop.

Stand down the acute contingency posture: with Hormuz reopened in practice (Iran FM 'operating normally', CENTCOM transit continuing), normalise routing while keeping war-risk cover on standby through the 60-day ceasefire window. Re-optimise vessel scheduling for lower freight (BDI near cycle lows); retain demurrage clauses until flows fully settle.

Manage the down-move with strategic accounts: the market has flipped from supply-scare to premium-unwind within days. Reaffirm reliability and OTIF, keep allocation transparent, and use dependability through the volatility to defend premium tolerance as buyers test for discounts.

Track: (1) durability of the ceasefire/Hormuz reopening — transit counts, CENTCOM vs Iran claims, nuclear-inspection dispute; (2) whether price holds the $3,260–$3,080 support band or extends lower; (3) EGA/ALBA restart timelines (the remaining supply swing); (4) USD/Fed path after the hawkish June FOMC; (5) China export pace (Fastmarkets +12–18%), Indonesian ramp & Section 232 (50%); (6) Guinea bauxite-curb risk. Headline risk is now skewed to re-escalation upside vs a supply-led drift lower.

bottom_line: The breakout has fully reversed and the correction is maturing. The 25-Jun official extended the slide to cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 — a fourth straight down-day and a ~4-month low — with the cash–3M curve deepening to a -$14 contango. The war premium is gone: the US-Iran ceasefire/MoU holds, Hormuz transit is normal, Brent is near a ~4-month low (~$74.7) and the USD holds a 13-month high. The structural deficit (EGA ~12-month restoration, ALBA partial, ~92%-Russian LME stock drawing to 308,225 t) still provides a floor, and the $4,000 upside scenario — contingent on a Strait shut >30 days — is off the table. US physical premiums stay tariff-inflated under the 50% Section 232 rate. Net: with the catalyst gone, the base case sits in the $3,080–$3,150 wave-C confluence (now tagged) on a firm dollar and rising China/Indonesia output, unless the Gulf re-escalates.
sources: London Metal Exchange via Westmetall — westmetall.com (25-Jun official cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 / stock 308,225 t; 24-Jun cash $3,150.00 / 3M $3,160.50 / stock 310,225 t)

Trading Economics — tradingeconomics.com/commodity/aluminum; aluminium at a ~4-month low; Brent ~$74.7 (~4-mo low); DXY ~101.5 (13-mo high); Fed Sept-hike odds ~63%
Reuters / CENTCOM (Iran FM 'operating normally'; CENTCOM transit continuing; 60-day Iran oil-export window)
Premiums (MJP / CIF Japan / EDU / US MW)

caveats: Report run Fri 26-Jun-2026. The latest confirmed LME official on Westmetall is the 25-Jun close (cash $3,132.00 / 3M $3,146.00 / stock 308,225 t); 'Previous' is the 24-Jun official ($3,150.00 / $3,160.50 / 310,225 t). The 26-Jun official had not yet been published at run time, so it is NOT shown — only verifiable figures are published.

The four-session slide to a ~4-month low reflects the full collapse of the Gulf war premium as the ceasefire/MoU held and Hormuz reopened in practice. Residual two-sided headline risk remains: a credible Gulf re-escalation could re-ignite the premium.

Macro/FX/energy values are 25-Jun reads (Westmetall FX / Trading Economics) — DXY ~101.5 (13-mo high), Brent ~$74.7 (~4-mo low), EUR/USD ~1.1338, USD/JPY ~160.1, gold ~$3,995. Re-validate against live feeds intraday.

Daily physical premium assessments (MJP, CIF Japan, EDU, Rotterdam billets, US Midwest) sit behind PRA paywalls (S&P Global Platts / Fastmarkets); levels shown are the latest public/derived references and should be verified against the subscription assessments.

All figures are PUBLIC, sourced data. No confidential, internal or Maaden-specific information is included; the only company-specific reference is public listed-company peer earnings (e.g., Maaden (1211)).

outlook.curve_note: Front-end backwardation has fully unwound: the cash–3M spread deepened to a -$14 contango as the Gulf de-escalation removed acute near-term scarcity. Consensus 2026 average drifts back toward the $3,200–3,500 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply is repaired. With the geopolitical catalyst gone, the curve is driven by the structural deficit (a floor) against rising China/Indonesia output and a firm dollar (the cap).

ai_analysis: What changed: the war premium has fully collapsed. After the contested 20-Jun Hormuz-reclosure declaration was repudiated (Iran FM 'operating normally', CENTCOM transit continuing) and Washington granted Iran a 60-day oil-export license, aluminium broke ~4.2% lower on 23-Jun to cash $3,263.50 / 3M $3,271.00 — a ~3-month low — as conflict-hedge longs unwound.

Term structure: the cash–3M curve rolled to a clearer contango (-$7.5 from -$1) as the reopening removed acute front-end scarcity; total LME stocks keep drawing (311,725 t) on the intact structural deficit.

Macro now dominant: on 24-Jun the USD extended to a 13-month high (DXY ~101.7) with CME FedWatch July-hike odds at ~36% (>70% by Sept) and Brent fell to a ~4-month low (~$75.9); with the geopolitical catalyst gone, dollar strength, rising China/Indonesia output and soft China demand set the near-term tone.

The remaining bull lever is supply, not corridor risk: EGA Al Taweelah (~12-month restore) and ALBA (partial) keep ~3 Mt impaired, underpinning a floor. Citi/Goldman's $4,000 upside scenario — contingent on a Strait shut >30 days — has been removed by the de-escalation.

Forward bias: base case (50%) = elevated but drifting lower ($3,100–$3,450) as the ceasefire holds and the deficit floors price; bear raised to 30% (durable de-escalation + China/Indonesia supply → $2,900–$3,150); bull cut to 20% (Gulf re-escalation). Near-term watch: the $3,260–$3,080 support band, EGA/ALBA restart timing, and the USD/Fed path.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): MJP Q2 settled near an 11-yr high (~$351.5/t, +80% q/q); CIF Japan spot easing further (~$345) as the Hormuz reopening removes the war premium. Q3 negotiations underway with de-escalation skewing the settlement down toward ~$275–320 (Platts/Fastmarkets).
European (EDU): ~$478/t, softening vs last month (~$519 avg) on weak demand but underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM.
US Midwest: MW US Transaction ~$2,420/t; duty-paid P1020A ~$2,055/t — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 tariff, tracking the lower LME via the tariff component.

Common thread: premiums spiked on the Gulf outage and are now easing as the strait reopens — unrepaired Gulf capacity keeps a floor while the policy (tariff/CBAM) overlay holds US/EU premiums structurally high.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$66 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$34); oversupply has roughly halved prices since Jan-25 — Guinea weighing export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$350/t) easing on Q2 oversupply; API region steady (~$305; FOB futures ~$307). Surplus building into H2; the Hormuz reopening removes the Gulf-logistics risk.
Bauxite: Guinea oversupply pushed CIF China to ~$66/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$34) — Guinea's export-curb threat (a >$100/dmt target) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.
Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite while caustic keeps a floor.
Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3 with the Gulf logistics risk now removed.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-traded — full transparency (3M ~$13,595/t, ~$6.34/lb, steadied on strong China data; COMEX near records on US tariff premium); alloy input for 2xxx & some 6xxx

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Bulk-freight proxy for bauxite/alumina; ~2,550, near a multi-month low; Capesize BCI ~3,700 — Trading Economics / HandyBulk / Bloomberg

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports & ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz. After the 17-Jun MoU and the repudiation of the 20-Jun reclosure declaration, transit has normalised — Iran's foreign ministry says shipping is 'operating normally', CENTCOM reports traffic continuing, and Iran shipped 30M+ bbl last week. A 60-day charge-free transit window is in force; corridor risk has receded to a tail risk.

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: Surcharges/war-risk premia easing as transit normalises; keep cover on standby through the 60-day ceasefire window in case of re-escalation.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround — hold on standby; no longer required day-to-day with Hormuz reopened, but retain as a contingency.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live until routing fully normalises

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — watch PPAs/carbon), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, ~$13,640/t).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.
Logistics has flipped back to NORMAL: the Hormuz reopening reverses the war-risk premium — normalise routing, keep cover on standby; BDI near a 2026-cycle low (~2,550) eases bulk costs.

Procurement read: power + carbon (coke/pitch) drive roughly half of conversion cost — prioritise these for hedging / forward contracting; alloying metals are smaller line items but China-concentrated.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at the $3,855 four-year high (early-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and an ABC correction is underway. Wave A bottomed at the 16-Jun $3,359 cycle low; wave B was a failed bounce that peaked at the 22-Jun $3,406 official (intraday spike to ~$3,607 on the contested reclosure) and was rejected at the .50. Wave C is now well advanced: the 25-Jun official ($3,132 cash / $3,146 3M) has pushed into the $3,080–$3,260 target confluence (the prior 4th-of-lesser-degree base / .382–.50 retrace of the whole advance), trading between the .382 ($3,261) and .50 ($3,078). Base case (~55%): wave C completes in the $3,080–$3,150 zone then bases. Bull alternate ~25% needs a weekly close back above the .236 ($3,488)/$3,666 to reopen the highs; deep bear ~20% targets the .618 ($2,894) on a sustained break below $3,080. The bearish-primary count is invalidated only on a sustained weekly close above $3,488. Confidence: medium-high — structure, contango and a firm dollar all align lower.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Short-term (daily): the decline off $3,855 unfolded as wave A into the 16-Jun $3,359 low; wave B bounced and spiked ~6% intraday to ~$3,607 on 22-Jun (the contested Hormuz reclosure) — the .50 retracement of the A-drop — and was rejected. Wave C is now confirmed and extending: price broke the wave-A low and the 25-Jun official printed $3,132 cash / $3,146 3M, a ~4-month low, tagging the $3,160–$3,080 target zone. Alternation holds — a sharp 5-wave A produced a sharp, news-driven B. Base case targets the $3,080 confluence into early-Aug, then basing. Bullish re-trigger: a daily close back above $3,488 (then $3,607) would question the count and reopen a $3,666/$3,855 retest. Bearish-floor invalidation: a sustained break under $3,080 opens $2,950/$2,894. Confidence: medium-high — the spike was sold and price keeps making lower lows on a firm dollar.

premium_drivers.d: Gulf regional production remains curtailed (EGA/ALBA ~3 Mt), but the corridor risk has ended: Iran's foreign ministry confirmed Hormuz shipping is 'operating normally', CENTCOM reports transit continuing, and a 60-day oil-export window is in force — the war premium has fully unwound.
50% tariff on full customs value (eff. 8-Jun-2026, through 2027) → US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (~$0.20–0.21/lb market premium + tariff) even as the LME falls.
EU import duty reverted to 6% (from 4%, 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM → European premiums structurally supported despite soft demand.
Gulf war-risk surcharges and freight easing as transit normalises and the closure declaration is repudiated; owners unwinding the spike in cover taken after 20-Jun.

so_what.line: Aluminium is consolidating near $3,400 after June's Hormuz-driven spike to a four-year high; near-term bias stays corrective unless $3,500 is reclaimed.
points: LME 3M ~$3,398 in mild contango — front-end tightness easing, though exchange stocks remain low (~315kt).
Physical premiums historically rich: Japan MJP $351/t (decade-high quarterly settlement); US Midwest tariff-inflated (~64% of LME) under Section 232.
China import window shut (LME above SHFE) — pressures ex-China premiums and favours Chinese exports.
Watch this week: Strait of Hormuz transit, the $3,260–3,080 technical support zone, and the dollar / Brent.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
